upperAir: The KOAK sounding shows a near-dry-adiabatic lapse rate of 7.66°C/km from surface to 850mb, indicating an unstable low-level atmosphere capable of supporting convective development — CAPE values in model data corroborate this (400–730 J/kg at multiple sites). The 850mb wind is 24 kts from 245° and strengthens to 34 kts at 500mb from 235°, indicating strong SW flow aloft with modest directional shear. Thermal ceiling is limited by cloud cover and instability rather than a classic subsidence inversion; the moist environment (850mb spread only 1.7°C) and precipitation reports suggest a convectively active day with limited usable thermal height.
aviationFlags: No active Bay Area AIRMETs. However, METARs paint a concerning picture: KHAF reporting -RA (light rain), BR (mist), gusts to 20 kts, and variable wind directions 230–290° through mid-afternoon — marine layer active and wet at the coast. KSFO reporting RAE (rain ended recently), BKN045, with SLP 1013.3 mb. KAPC reporting BKN020/BKN045 with recent rain. KRHV showing SCT028/BKN045 with gusts to 17 kts at 2111Z. Widespread low cloud, recent precipitation, and gusty conditions across all reporting stations indicate a post-frontal or active frontal passage scenario — not a normal flying day.

sites.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — inland ridge, S/SW-facing, thermal and ridge lift site
sites.howItWorks: Ed Levin 1750 launches into valley thermals and upslope flow on the south-facing ridge. Ideal conditions are NW 8–15 kts at launch with active thermals developing by late morning; sea breeze pushes in from the west by early afternoon.
sites.todaySetup: Today is a NO FLY day at Ed Levin 1750. The deciding factors are 100% cloud cover persisting all day, CAPE values of 400–550 J/kg indicating convective instability rather than clean thermals, and precipitation probability reaching 50–55% by early afternoon. Winds at 10m are 12–15 kts from the SSW (195–254°), which is cross to unfavorable for this launch, and 80m winds hit 17–19 kts. The 850mb wind at 24 kts SW means conditions aloft are aggressive and the cloud deck is locked in with moist air (850mb spread 1.7°C). KRHV METARs confirm gusty SW flow with broken ceilings locally at 2,800–4,500 ft. With convective CAPE, rain probability exceeding 50% by 1 PM, and zero sky visibility through the flyable window, there is no safe launch opportunity today.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 100% cloud cover, 12–15 kts SSW at launch, CAPE 200–460 J/kg, precip probability 17–33%. Completely socked in with unstable air — no launch.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds 12–15 kts swinging SW to WSW, CAPE 400–420 J/kg, precip probability 31–55%, still 100% overcast. Rain possible, convective threat increasing — definitely no fly.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds 11–13 kts WSW, clouds partially breaking to 82–97%, but precip probability still 16–37% and instability remains. Marginal sky but convective risk lingers too long to trust.

verdict.hazards: 100% overcast all morning, CAPE 400–550 J/kg, 50%+ rain probability by early afternoon, gusty SW cross-winds, convective instability

sites.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — lower training hill, SW-facing, foot-launch thermal site
sites.howItWorks: The 600 ft training hill at Ed Levin works on light thermic days with gentle NW-to-W flow. It is a benign site in calm conditions but completely unsuitable in strong, gusty, or convective weather.
sites.todaySetup: Today is a NO FLY day at the 600 ft hill for all the same reasons as the 1750, compounded by the lower elevation offering no escape from turbulent air. Winds at 10m are 8–15 kts from the SSW (195–269°), which is directly cross to unfavorable, and 80m winds reach 13–18 kts. CAPE values are actually higher at this elevation — 550–610 J/kg at midday — indicating the strongest convective instability is in the lower valley. With 100% cloud cover, rain probability exceeding 50% by early afternoon, and gusty cross-winds, this hill should not be used today. The lower launch height means less reaction time to any sudden thermal or gust event.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 100% overcast, 8–15 kts SSW, CAPE 130–550 J/kg, precip 18–31%. Cross-wind, overcast, unstable — no launch.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds 12–15 kts SW-to-WSW, CAPE 90–610 J/kg, precip 28–52%, overcast. Peak instability window — absolutely no fly.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds 9–13 kts WSW to W, cloud cover easing slightly to 94–100%, precip 13–21%. Still too gusty and unstable for the training hill.

verdict.hazards: SSW cross-wind, 100% overcast, CAPE 550–610 J/kg at midday, 50%+ rain probability, convective turbulence risk

sites.name: Mussel Rock
sites.locationDescriptor: Daly City — coastal ridge, W/NW-facing, marine layer and sea breeze ridge site
sites.howItWorks: Mussel Rock is a pure ridge soaring site that needs steady W-to-NW onshore wind at 10–20 kts to sustain lift along the coastal bluff. Marine layer and sea breeze are the drivers; thermal activity is secondary and often suppressed by the marine environment.
sites.todaySetup: Today is a NO FLY day at Mussel Rock. The coastal conditions are actively hostile: KHAF METAR reported light rain, mist (BR), and visibility as low as 1.75 SM earlier this morning, with gusts to 20 kts from 260–280°. The Half Moon Bay buoy shows 17.5 kts from 240° with 2.8m waves at 9-second period and SST of 14°C — cold water driving a robust marine layer. While the afternoon models show winds easing to 11–16 kts from 260–270° (favorable direction) with partial cloud clearing, the morning precipitation and low visibility suggest the marine layer has depth and will be slow to fully clear. CAPE values of 230–730 J/kg are unusually high for a coastal site and indicate embedded convective energy that could produce sudden rotor turbulence and gusty wind swings. The combination of recent precipitation, gusty onshore flow, and convective CAPE makes this unsuitable even for experienced ridge pilots today.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: KHAF reporting rain/mist/BR with gusts 18–20 kts and visibility under 2 SM. 95–100% cloud cover, CAPE 260–510 J/kg. Active precipitation and near-zero visibility — hard no.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Models show partial clearing to 40–99% cloud cover, winds 11–16 kts from 246–264° (good direction), but CAPE remains 310–730 J/kg and precip probability 23–36%. Rotor and convective risk not cleared.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Best window on paper — 12–16 kts W, 5–31% cloud cover, CAPE 200–320 J/kg, precip only 4–12%. Direction and speed are flyable but convective instability lingers and it's a post-frontal environment; conditions could deteriorate rapidly.

verdict.hazards: Rain and mist this morning per KHAF METAR, 17.5 kts offshore with 2.8m waves, CAPE 230–730 J/kg with convective rotor risk, gusts to 20 kts, post-frontal instability all day

sites.name: Mission Peak
sites.locationDescriptor: Fremont — inland ridge/thermal site, W-facing launch, strong sea breeze exposure
sites.howItWorks: Mission Peak works on thermal days with a building NW-to-W sea breeze that provides sustained ridge lift by mid-afternoon. Thermals develop from the east-facing slopes in the morning; sea breeze front arrival from the bay can create abrupt wind shift and turbulence if timing is off.
sites.todaySetup: Today is a NO FLY day at Mission Peak. The atmospheric story is nearly identical to Ed Levin — this site shares the same regional airmass with 100% cloud cover through midday, CAPE of 400–460 J/kg, and precip probability reaching 52% by 1 PM. Winds are 12–15 kts from SSW (199–254°) which is directly cross to the W-facing launch. KAPC is reporting BKN020/BKN045 with recent rain, confirming the inland valley is not clearing. The 850mb SW flow at 24 kts would produce significant mechanical turbulence over the ridge terrain even if the thermals were clean, which they are not under a convective overcast. No window opens today.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 100% overcast, 10–16 kts SSW (cross to unfavorable), CAPE 160–460 J/kg, precip 17–32%. No launch in these conditions.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds 12–15 kts SSW-to-WSW, CAPE 400–420 J/kg, precip probability 31–52%, 80–100% overcast. Peak convective threat window — no fly.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds ease to 11–13 kts WSW, some cloud clearing to 82–97%, precip probability drops to 13–21%. Direction improving but instability still present and ceiling still limiting.

verdict.hazards: 100% overcast, CAPE 400–460 J/kg, 52% precip probability by early afternoon, SSW cross-wind at launch, KAPC confirming low broken ceilings and recent rain

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Monday looks significantly better. HRRR/GFS agree on light NW winds 5–10 kts, cloud cover dropping to 28–78%, CAPE under 130 J/kg, and near-zero precip. BL rises to 985 m by afternoon. A light NW thermal day is possible — best window likely 12–4 PM. Winds may be slightly light (6–10 kts at 80m) so thermals could be weak but usable for P2/P3.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Monday similar to 1750 — light winds 2–7 kts with NW-to-W flow, clouds clearing through the day, no precip. Very light conditions suit the training hill well. Could be a calm, pleasant soaring day for P2 pilots in the 11 AM–3 PM window.

tomorrow.name: Mussel Rock
tomorrow.analysis: Monday shows light variable winds early (2–9 kts from NNW/NW), partially cloudy, no precip. Tomorrow.io shows 8–9 kts NW by morning with gusts to 11 kts — marginal for ridge soaring. GFS shows 8–12 kts NW by afternoon, which is borderline usable. Watch for wind to build enough to sustain ridge lift by mid-afternoon; may be a MARGINAL day.

tomorrow.name: Mission Peak
tomorrow.analysis: Monday mirrors Ed Levin — light 5–7 kts NW-to-W, clouds partially clearing, no rain. Afternoon BL to 985 m. Expect weak-to-moderate thermals if sun gets through. Best window 12–4 PM. MARGINAL to FLY depending on how quickly clouds thin.

day2.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day2.analysis: Tuesday (Apr 14) looks clean — GFS shows 3–11 kts NW/W, mostly clear skies (6–78% cloud, clearing by noon), zero CAPE, zero precip, BL reaching 1220–1455 m by afternoon. This could be a solid thermal day with a classic sea breeze cycle. Best window 11 AM–4 PM.

day2.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day2.analysis: Tuesday similar to 1750 — light NW winds 4–7 kts, clearing skies, BL to 1220 m. A good training hill day with light thermals and manageable sea breeze in the afternoon.

day2.name: Mussel Rock
day2.analysis: Tuesday shows 9–15 kts W-to-NW (270–293°) with clearing skies by noon and zero precip. SST patterns suggest marine layer should be thin. This looks like a genuine FLY day for Mussel Rock — afternoon ridge session should be viable with 10–14 kts onshore flow.

day2.name: Mission Peak
day2.analysis: Tuesday GFS shows 5–10 kts W-to-NW, mostly clear by midday, BL to 1395 m, zero CAPE, zero precip. Looks like a quality thermal day. P2 and P3 should find a solid 11 AM–4 PM window.

day3.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day3.analysis: No Day 3 (Apr 15) data available in the provided forecast window. Based on the trend of improving high pressure after the Sunday frontal passage, conditions are expected to remain favorable through mid-week. Monitor updates.

day3.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day3.analysis: Day 3 data not provided. Trend toward high pressure and light NW flow suggests continued flyable conditions mid-week, consistent with a post-frontal pattern.

day3.name: Mussel Rock
day3.analysis: Day 3 data not provided. Post-frontal NW flow pattern typically benefits Mussel Rock with steady onshore winds. Monitor the marine layer depth as high pressure builds.

day3.name: Mission Peak
day3.analysis: Day 3 data not provided. Post-frontal clearing and NW flow expected to persist. Likely another thermal day similar to Tuesday if the pattern holds.

watchlist: Sunday is a complete washout — frontal/post-frontal precipitation, 100% overcast, and CAPE 400–730 J/kg make all sites NO FLY today
KHAF reporting rain, mist, and visibility under 2 SM this morning — coastal marine layer is deep and wet
CAPE values of 400–730 J/kg across all sites indicate embedded convective instability and potential rotor/turbulence even if it stays dry
Monday may see very light winds (2–6 kts) — good for training hill but potentially too light for sustained soaring at 1750 ft or Mission Peak
Tuesday looks like the best day of the stretch — watch for high pressure confirmation and BL heights reaching 1200–1400 m
tldr: Today is a hard pass at all four sites — a frontal system brought rain, low ceilings, gusty SW cross-winds, and CAPE up to 730 J/kg. Nobody should be in the air Sunday. Monday lightens up but winds may be too calm; Tuesday looks like the real reward with NW flow, clearing skies, and BL heights pushing 1400 m.
